app.title: Hipótesis
controls.title: Control Strategies
controls.description: Enable and configure population control measures to see their projected effect.
controls.lethal.label: Lethal Control
controls.lethal.description: Humane euthanasia applied by wildlife specialists. The only method modeled to achieve population reduction at scale.
controls.sterilization.label: Sterilization
controls.sterilization.description: Surgical sterilization of captured individuals. Logistically intensive: each animal requires weeks of preparation.
controls.sterilization.note.males: Note: sterilizing males has negligible effect while fertile males remain in the population. Female sterilization drives the birth rate reduction.
controls.displacement.label: Displacement
controls.displacement.description: Relocation to zoos or sanctuaries. Limited by worldwide capacity and complex CITES quarantine requirements.
chart.title: Hippo Population Projection
chart.xAxis.label: Year
chart.yAxis.label: Population
stats.tooltip.currentPopulation: Population estimates disagree: official counts put the 2023 population near 169 (Cornare) rising to ~200 by 2026, while the fitted model of Castelblanco-Martínez et al. (2023, Scientific Reports) gives ~91 in 2022 / ~131 in 2026. This app uses the official counts.
stats.tooltip.populationIn2060: Estimated number of individuals in 2060 under the currently selected control strategies.
stats.tooltip.yearsToCapacity: Year the population is projected to cross 1,500 individuals. This is a milestone, not an ecological carrying capacity: Castelblanco-Martínez et al. (2023, Scientific Reports) model growth as density-independent (~9.6%/yr) and find no indication that hippos approach a carrying capacity in the coming decades — the Magdalena basin could physically hold tens of thousands.
stats.tooltip.projectionHippoYears: Total area under the projection curve (2026–2060). Each unit represents the environmental impact of one individual present for one full year — an aggregate measure of the total impact of the invasive process over the analyzed period.
waste.title: Ecological footprint
waste.subtitle: Cumulative dung these hippos would produce over 2026–2060, shown filling Hacienda Nápoles (~22 km²) — the estate they escaped from. The outline is a schematic size reference, not a precise boundary.
waste.figureAlt: Schematic outline of Hacienda Nápoles filling with dung emojis in proportion to the waste generated.
waste.tooltip.mass: Cumulative wet dung by mass, from the area under the projection curve (hippo-years) × ~10 kg per hippo per day (wet mass; Stears et al. 2018, PNAS).
waste.tooltip.pools: The same waste by volume, expressed in standard Olympic swimming pools (2,500 m³ each), assuming a water-like density of ~1,000 kg/m³.
waste.tooltip.area: Ground area the waste would cover if spread uniformly 1 cm deep. Hacienda Nápoles — the estate the hippos escaped from — covers roughly 22 km².
waste.nitrogen: Tonnes of nitrogen loaded to water
waste.tooltip.grass: Cumulative terrestrial grass grazed, from hippo-years × ~45 kg per hippo per night (Stears et al. 2018, PNAS — the midpoint of 40–50 kg). Colombian hippos are assumed to eat like African ones; this has not been measured locally. Football-pitch equivalent assumes ~2 kg of fresh grass per m² of pasture.
waste.tooltip.nitrogen: Cumulative nitrogen carried into water via dung and urine, from hippo-years × ~45 kg N per hippo per year (derived from Subalusky et al. 2015). This is a loading rate, not a volume of water 'contaminated' — it drives eutrophication, cyanobacteria blooms and oxygen crashes, as documented in the lakes near Hacienda Nápoles (Shurin et al. 2020, Ecology).
about.title: About Hipótesis
about.intro: Hipótesis is an open educational tool that visualizes population growth projections for the invasive hippopotamus population in Colombia's Magdalena river basin. The model is based on peer-reviewed ecological research.
about.model.title: Population Model
about.model.description: The historical curve (1981–2026) is not simulated: it interpolates documented census estimates, which disagree — official counts reach ~200 by 2026, while the fitted model of Castelblanco-Martínez et al. (2023) gives ~131. This app uses the official counts. From 2026 the projection uses a density-independent, sex-structured birth model at ~9.6%/year net growth, matching that same study, which found no indication the hippos approach a carrying capacity in the coming decades (the Magdalena basin could physically hold tens of thousands). Growth is therefore exponential — the 1,500 line on the chart is a mid-century milestone, not an ecological ceiling — and control measures are what bend the curve.
about.model.historical.title: Phase 1 — Historical curve (1981–2026)
about.model.historical.description: This phase is not simulated. It expands a handful of documented census counts into a yearly series by interpolating geometrically between consecutive anchors, so the curve passes exactly through every published estimate and holds a constant growth ratio in between.
about.model.historical.formula: Population in year y, interpolated between two consecutive census anchors a and b.
about.model.historical.note: Because each segment is fitted to its own pair of counts, the implied growth rate is not constant: the official anchors imply about 12%/yr for 2007–2012, 16%/yr for 2012–2019, 21%/yr for 2019–2023 and 6%/yr for 2023–2026. Those swings are averages over each segment, and they are a large part of why the two census sources have drifted so far apart by 2026.
about.model.projection.title: Phase 2 — Projection (2026–2060)
about.model.projection.description: From 2026 onward the app runs a discrete-time model with a one-year step. The state is three numbers: the population and the standing stocks of sterilized females and males. Each year births are added, natural deaths and control removals are subtracted, and the sterilized stocks are carried forward.
about.model.projection.formula.fertile: Fertile-female fraction — the share of females still able to breed. Sterilization enters the model only here.
about.model.projection.formula.populatio: Population update. Births scale with the fertile fraction; removals do not.
about.model.projection.formula.sterilize: Sterilized stocks: scaled by the year's survival ratio, topped up by that year's surgeries, and capped at half the population.
about.model.projection.note: Removals are assumed random with respect to sterilization status, so the sterilized stocks shrink in the same proportion as the population. The caps at N/2 encode the 1:1 sex-ratio assumption — you cannot sterilize more females than exist. Sterilized males are tracked but never enter the birth term: a single fertile male can service many females, so removing males from the breeding pool changes nothing until nearly all of them are gone.
about.model.symbols.title: Symbols
about.model.symbols.population: Population at the start of year t
about.model.symbols.sterilizedFemales: Sterilized females alive in year t
about.model.symbols.sterilizedMales: Sterilized males alive in year t
about.model.symbols.lethal: Individuals removed by lethal control per year
about.model.symbols.survivalRatio: Survival ratio applied to the sterilized stocks
about.model.implications.title: What the equations imply
about.model.implications.growth.title: Left alone, the population doubles every ~7.6 years
about.model.implications.growth.descript: With no controls the fertile fraction stays at 1 and the recurrence collapses to pure geometric growth at the net rate b − d = 0.096 per year. The doubling time follows directly. This is why the curve looks almost flat for a decade and then climbs steeply: nothing about the growth rate changes, only the size of the base it is applied to. Left alone, 200 hippos in 2026 become roughly 4,500 by 2060.
about.model.implications.removal.title: Removals have a break-even point, and it is unstable
about.model.implications.removal.descrip: With removals but no sterilization the update is linear, and it has exactly one fixed point. Below it the population collapses toward zero; above it the population still grows without bound. Because the annual multiplier exceeds 1, that equilibrium is unstable — there is no steady state to settle into, only a threshold to be on the right side of. At the 2026 population of 200, break-even sits near 19 animals a year: 19 leaves the population still creeping upward, to about 245 by 2060; 20 turns it over and drives it down to about 20 by 2060; 21 eradicates it by 2053. Two animals a year separate slowly winning from slowly losing, and because the equilibrium is unstable that gap widens over time instead of closing.
about.model.implications.sterilization.t: Sterilization alone needs over 80% of the females
about.model.implications.sterilization.d: Sterilization removes no animals; it only shrinks the birth term through the fertile fraction. The population declines only once births fall below natural deaths, which requires the fertile fraction to drop under d ⁄ b ≈ 0.172 — that is, more than 82.8% of all females sterilized, and kept sterilized while the population keeps growing underneath. It is a moving target, because the denominator rises every year. At the simulator's lowest rate of 4 females a year the threshold is never reached by 2060 and the curve merely flattens; at its highest rate of 8 a year it is crossed in 2044 and the population finally turns over. Male sterilization does not move this number at all.
about.model.assumptions.title: Assumptions and limitations
about.model.assumptions.intro: The model is deliberately simple — simple enough to run instantly in the browser and to be read off this page. Every simplification below makes it less faithful than the published studies it is calibrated against.
about.model.assumptions.sexRatio: The sex ratio is fixed at 1:1, so females always number N ⁄ 2. Real populations drift from this, especially under selective removal.
about.model.assumptions.noAgeStructure: No age structure. Castelblanco-Martínez et al. use a Leslie matrix with age classes and stage-specific survival; this model applies one birth rate and one death rate to every animal, so it cannot reproduce the lags a young or ageing cohort creates.
about.model.assumptions.noDensityDepende: No density dependence and no carrying capacity. This is deliberate and follows the source study, which found no sign of the population approaching a ceiling in the coming decades — but it means the projection has no brake whatsoever. Extended far enough, it grows without limit.
about.model.assumptions.noSpatial: No space. The hippos are a single well-mixed pool, while the real population is spreading along the Magdalena. Dispersal, not just numbers, is what drives conflict with people.
about.model.assumptions.deterministic: Deterministic. There is no environmental or demographic stochasticity, so the app draws one line rather than a confidence band. Real projections at these population sizes carry wide uncertainty.
about.model.assumptions.randomRemoval: Removals are random with respect to sterilization status. In practice a program that has already sterilized an animal is unlikely to then cull it.
about.model.assumptions.sterilizationIns: Sterilization is instantaneous, permanent and effective in the same year. There is no surgical failure rate, no recovery period and no capture mortality.
about.model.assumptions.malesInert: Male sterilization has no effect on births by construction. That is a modelling choice rather than a measured result, though it matches the biology of a polygynous species.
about.model.assumptions.annualStep: The time step is one year, and births are applied before removals within each step. A continuous-time formulation would give slightly different numbers.
about.model.assumptions.continuous: Populations are continuous rather than whole animals — the model can hold 3.4 hippos. This only matters near zero, where a genuinely extinct population would round down.
about.impact.title: Environmental impact estimates
about.impact.description: The ecological-footprint figures scale the area under the projected population curve (hippo-years) by per-hippo rates from the literature. Waste assumes ~10 kg of wet dung per hippo per day, shown spread 1 cm deep over Hacienda Nápoles (~22 km²), the estate the hippos escaped from. Grass grazed uses ~45 kg of vegetation per night, expressed as football pitches assuming ~2 kg of fresh grass per m². The water figure is nitrogen delivered (~45 kg N per hippo per year) — a loading rate that drives eutrophication, not a volume of water 'contaminated'. Rates are measured on African hippos and assumed to apply to the Colombian population, which has not been independently measured.
about.references.title: References
about.references.castelblanco2021: Castelblanco-Martínez, D.N., et al. (2021). A hippo in the room: Predicting the persistence and dispersion of an invasive mega-vertebrate in Colombia, South America. Biological Conservation, 253, 108923.
about.references.stears2018: Stears, K., McCauley, D.J., Finlay, J.C., et al. (2018). Effects of the hippopotamus on the chemistry and ecology of a changing watershed. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 115(22).
about.references.subalusky2015: Subalusky, A.L., Dutton, C.L., Rosi-Marshall, E.J., & Post, D.M. (2015). The hippopotamus conveyor belt: vectors of carbon and nutrients from terrestrial grasslands to aquatic systems in sub-Saharan Africa. Freshwater Biology, 60(3).
about.references.dutton2018: Dutton, C.L., Subalusky, A.L., Hamilton, S.K., Rosi, E.J., & Post, D.M. (2018). Organic matter loading by hippopotami causes subsidy overload resulting in downstream hypoxia and fish kills. Nature Communications, 9, 1951.
about.references.shurin2020: Shurin, J.B., Aranguren-Riaño, N., Duque Negro, D., et al. (2020). Ecosystem effects of the world's largest invasive animal. Ecology, 101(5), e02991.
about.references.data: Boundary data: Colombian department and river polygons from open GeoJSON datasets (DANE). The Hacienda Nápoles waste-reference outline is a schematic footprint near the real estate location; its ~22 km² area is the load-bearing figure, not the drawn shape. Reference values: Olympic pool (2,500 m³) and FIFA football pitch (105 × 68 m) by definition.
footer.disclaimer: Population projections are estimates based on a simplified mathematical model. They are intended for educational purposes and do not constitute official scientific advice.
map.title: Spatial distribution
